data.title: Wolters Kluwer: A High-Quality Company Discounted By AI
data.description:
Wolters Kluwer (OTC:WOLTF) is a Dutch software and professional services company. Wolters Kluwer has an excellent track record of growth, profitability, and return on capital. Due to the fears about disruption by artificial intelligence, the shares have declined heavily. Despite the fears and weaker U.S. dollar, Wolters Kluwer has been posting strong revenue and profit growth, backed by an excellent track record, dividends, and buybacks.

Company Overview

Wolters Kluwer posts an annual revenue of approximately €6 billion, of which 84% is recurring. The company has five diverse divisions serving health, tax, accounting, risk & compliance, and legal professionals. Corporate Performance & ESG is the newest division formed in March 2023 by combining four existing software businesses under the divisional banner. The company has 21,700 employees.

The company offers expert information, software, and services. For example, one-fifth of its revenue comes from recurring software sales. Geographically, 64% of its sales came from North America, 28% from Europe, and 8% from Asia Pacific and the rest of the world. Wolters Kluwer typically acquires 2-5 businesses each year. Due to growth by acquisitions, it operates under many different brands.

In 2024, Wolters Kluwer enjoyed 6% organic growth and 11% adjusted EPS growth. In the first nine months of 2025, the company reported 7% organic growth, and adjusted operating profit increased 15% in constant currency. Wolters Kluwer reaffirmed the 2025 outlook, including margin expansion, flat free cash flow, and mid- to high-single-digit EPS growth.

Threat of AI

Investors are afraid that AI will eat into Wolters Kluwer’s customers and applications. The tech giants and AI-native companies are targeting the most lucrative industries, such as healthcare. OpenAI and Anthropic have recently launched their health products for medical applications. Microsoft’s Dragon Copilot is another potential threat. These three are solving other types of problems; generally speaking, their primary task, for now, is different from what Wolters Kluwer’s UpToDate does.

Wolters Kluwer’s medical product called UpToDate brings in 10% of the company’s revenue. It has 3 million users and 7600 expert contributors. To some extent, UpToDate includes proprietary content and is reviewed by professionals. This should create trust and additional value for the users. Since Wolters Kluwer’s customers and users are experts in their respective fields, one could argue that the integrity of the system and accuracy of the information play a crucial role. Its subscription services enjoy 90% renewal rates.

Naturally, Wolters Kluwer has developed and is developing its own AI-powered products and integrating AI functionalities into existing ones. In 2024 the company spent €660 million on product development. On the 8th of December, Wolters Kluwer organized an “Investor Teach-In” on artificial intelligence, demonstrating its experience and progress in AI. It has been working with AI for over a decade and, for example, launched UpToDate Generative Labs in October 2023.

In addition to product development, many of its recent acquisitions have been companies that are focused on AI applications. Most recently, Wolters Kluwer acquired Libra GmbH, which provides AI technology for legal professionals. In the beginning of 2025, the company made a large acquisition of Brightflag for €425 million in the same application area. Furthermore, in the past decade, Wolters Kluwer has transformed itself, which made the majority of its revenue from print materials into a digital world. AI is another potential disruptor, but Wolters Kluwer is actively managing it.

Excellent Track Record of Profitable Growth

The share price of Wolters Kluwer has halved since its peak one year ago. The last time the stock was trading at its current level was in mid-2022. Since then the company has grown its revenue from €5 billion to €6 billion and net income from €820 million to €1.1 billion. At the same time, margins have inched higher.

Wolters Kluwer is a highly profitable business. Its EBIT and FCF margins have both averaged 20% in the past decade. Revenue has grown at a 5% rate, and earnings per share have grown at a pace of 11%. Wolters Kluwer has a strong balance sheet. Its net debt to EBITDA ratio stands at 2.2x. The indebtedness is slightly higher than usual due to several acquisitions and buybacks. In 2024, the ratio stood at 1.6x.

Wolters Kluwer will face a change of leadership in 2026 as long-tenured CEO Nancy McKinstry retires. She will be replaced by the CEO of the Health division, Stacey Caywood. Her career at Wolters Kluwer started in 1992. The company’s future has been and will continue to be in experienced hands. Recently, a British quality-focused fund, Fundsmith, established a position in Wolters Kluwer. Although the fund has underperformed for many years, an endorsement by its famous fund manager, Terry Smith, is a promising sign.

Valuation is Attractive by Historically Conservative Assumptions

The share price decline has compressed Wolters Kluwer’s multiples significantly below their historical averages. Its forward P/E multiple stands at 16x while it has averaged 23x over the past decade. P/FCF-multiple is 14.2x, and the 10-year average is over 20x. Its financial performance, margins, and returns on capital are all at their highest levels in history. Markets have priced in something that has not yet materialized. Analysts still expect earnings to grow at an annual pace of 10% in the next couple of years.

Analysts are expecting earnings per share of €5.26 for 2025. If we assume that the threat of AI is overestimated and Wolters Kluwer will be able to grow earnings at an annual pace of 8%, two percentage points lower than historically, the stock appears to be trading below its fair value. An ROIC of 18% and the assumed earnings growth should justify a P/E multiple higher than 20x. A 10% discount rate and a dividend growing in line with earnings result in a fair value of €102. An 8% discount rate would lead to a fair value of €121.

There are several other companies and stocks that have experienced similar sell-offs as Wolters Kluwer. Gartner (IT) and RELX (RELX) are fairly good peers. A comparison gives a rather neutral picture of Wolters Kluwer’s valuation. Based on earnings, the stock appears attractively valued, taking into account the prevailing financial performance. That could indicate that the price decline has gone a bit too far.

The Company is Returning Cash at an Accelerated Pace

Wolters Kluwer pays a semi-annual dividend. For the fiscal year 2024, the company paid a total dividend of €2.33 per share (€0.83+€1.5). For the fiscal year 2025, it is likely to pay the final dividend in May. The payout ratio stands comfortably below 50%. It has maintained or increased the dividend for 30 years, and it aims to pay a progressive dividend. The dividend increases have continued for nine consecutive years at an annual rate of 10%.

In November 2025, the company completed a €1 billion share repurchase program and has been authorized for an additional €200 million of buybacks. That represents 5.7% of the less than €21 billion market cap. The company has executed the program throughout 2025. Between 2020 and 2024, the company has bought back 35.6 million shares. In hindsight, it paid dearly for 6.4 million shares in 2024 as repurchases were made at an average price of €149. Now, the company is delightfully taking a stance behind the depressed share price with additional repurchases.

Conclusion

Wolters Kluwer is a high-quality company facing a new threat that is potentially overestimated by the market. The company has an excellent track record of profitable growth and successful transformations and is led by experienced leaders. In addition to rebound potential, the shares offer a decent dividend yield, and earnings are boosted by the buybacks. Although the threat of AI requires close monitoring, the valuation appears attractive.

The company will report its full year 2025 results on February 25th.

data.title: The eIPP Effect: Why Archer Aviation's Regulatory Path Now Looks Clearer Than Ever
data.description:
Thesis: A clearer regulatory framework

Archer Aviation (ACHR) has made a move to launch air taxi trials across quite a few U.S. cities under the White House’s newly established eVTOL Integration Pilot Program (eIPP). It's a pretty big inflexion point for both the company and the broader advanced air mobility space in general. Now, the first thing this does for Archer is that it aligns their initial commercial plans with a federal framework. It’s actually set to be the first of its kind at scale. A coordinated, federally sanctioned pathway that can move eVTOL aircraft from testing/certification into a real-world environment like city-level operations. And the best thing is that it comes with a much clearer regulatory framework for Archer to follow, albeit not fully finalised.

You see, the program is established by executive order and led by the U.S. Department of Transportation in coordination with the FAA. So it’s designed to accelerate these early air taxi deployments by bringing together federal regulators with the state and local governments to see what they really want from U.S. eVTOL manufacturers before launching in public/private partnerships. Prior to having the eIPP, there was a lot of regulatory uncertainty for Archer, centred around how eVTOLs would actually operate once certified. They had multiple questions around airspace access and how they would interact with their existing air traffic control systems. And that's because eVTOL integration is still at a really early stage and a whole new technology for many of these cities' local authorities. I think they want the Midnight to come to their cities and see the appeal/upside in what Archer can bring; they don’t want to be left with room for error. There are vertiport infrastructure standards, emergency response coordination, even noise rules, and local government approvals that remain pretty fragmented and largely untested at scale. So companies like Archer, which can design and certify aircraft, now have a unified mechanism to validate their models in live urban environments under this program.

The eIPP essentially addresses the gap by allowing a few selected companies, like Archer, to conduct some controlled, early operations with explicit federal support. So this should give the regulators an opportunity to observe how the Midnight can operate in real-world performance. I think once they see this, a lot of questions can be answered, and we will see these cities prepare their infrastructure and policies in parallel with what players like Archer are doing. And keep in mind that this effect should become amplified since it is being combined with the DOT’s newly released National AAM Strategy. They, on the other hand, want to synchronise the whole certification process, from building operations to infrastructure development, and eventually early deployment, so it brings it all into a single national roadmap rather than a series of disconnected approvals, which we used to see Archer hop from. So now, I see Archer having a bit less execution risk since they now have clarity on the ‘last mile’ between aircraft certification and commercial service. The timing is also critical since Archer are nearing some pretty key certification milestones and planning for operations ahead of the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics. I see it as the eIPP and National AAM Strategy effectively converting years of policy ambiguity into an actionable launch framework for the company.

Archer is also participating here through multiple public/private partnerships, which include operations in California, Texas, Florida, Georgia, and New York. So they’re clearly going with a deliberate, city-by-city approach to market entry that prioritises local infrastructure readiness. Their goal here is to gain operational credibility first on these stages and improve their public safety image. For example, if we take the exclusive application with Huntington Beach, it goes to show how strong Archer’s position in the Los Angeles region is and also directly supports its role as the Official Air Taxi Provider for the 2028 Olympic Games. So there's a clear path from here, from pilot programs to globally visible commercial operations. Strategically speaking, we want the initiative to help set up Archer not just as an aircraft manufacturer, but as an operator tied to federal, state, and municipal planning. I'm expecting this to give them a first-mover advantage whilst still giving the image to investors and regulators that the industry has moved decisively from just experimental testing into execution. The main goal being show us there are early revenue-generating flight operations taking place.

The role of Nvidia IGX Thor

As you know, Archer has just announced at CES 2026 that it’s set to build the next gen of aviation-focused artificial intelligence by using Nvidia’s IGX Thor platform. It's a pretty big step in the right direction in the maturation of eVTOL. I also expect the platform to significantly advance its air mobility tech, as I'll explain. For those that don’t know, the IGX Thor platform is a pretty high-performance, safety-certified edge AI computing system. It's specifically designed for real-time, mission-critical environments, which means it can deliver massive amounts of AI processing power directly onboard vehicles/machines. So this should enable things like advanced perception, sensor fusion, decision-making, and predictive analytics with extremely low latency. It’s also built to meet safety and determinism requirements, which makes it ideal for domains such as aviation, robotics, and autonomous systems, where Archer just can’t afford failures since AI has to operate continuously under strict operational constraints.

Now, in using this edge AI, Archer is hoping to get a core, safety-critical system underpinning pilot awareness, not just a peripheral enhancement. What I mean by this is that Archer is now essentially designing their aircraft so that AI is embedded at the heart of how the actual aircraft operates, rather than just some add-on used only for limited features or post-flight analysis. Now, these AI systems are tipped to continuously process data from sensors to give navigation systems and airspace inputs in real time during flights. For example, the basic things would, of course, be detecting potential conflicts, such as nearby aircraft, unexpected obstacles, or updates on weather conditions. They also come in the form of clear, prioritised alerts before a human might notice them. Now, in terms of airspace integration, AI can also dynamically adjust an eVTOL’s routing and timing to operate far more safely within a crowded urban airspace. This aspect should help Archer’s aircraft coexist with helicopters/commercial jets whilst also complying with air traffic control requirements. The planned deployment for the system is at Hawthorne Municipal Airport, which is now owned by Archer and tipped to be the operational heart of its Los Angeles air taxi network. So in my mind, this should create a rare, vertically integrated test environment where Archer can use it in real-world flight operations, manufacturing and pilot training.

The competition

Now, under the new regulatory framework created by eIPP and the DOT’s National AAM Strategy, I think we’re seeing Archer’s go-to-market approach starting to meaningfully diverge from, and, in some respects, de-risk itself relative to their competition, such as Joby Aviation (JOBY). Archer is pursuing here for a federally aligned, city-partnered rollout strategy. So they want to embed themselves directly into public-private partnerships that involve the likes of the DOT, FAA, and municipal governments from the outset. When you look at Joby’s more vertically integrated, operator-first model, it's somewhat of a contrast. Joby has historically emphasised internal control over aircraft development, operations, and services. And they often advance through bilateral FAA engagement rather than a coordinated, city-led federal framework. It's also no secret that Joby has made pretty strong progress on certification and operational readiness, but now with Archer’s eIPP participation, they also have a pathway for validation.

Most importantly, Archer’s strategy now aligns very closely with how regulators and cities want eVTOLs to be introduced. They need them coming in incrementally, transparently, and with shared responsibility for things like infrastructure, safety, and public acceptance. The alignment is even stronger when you consider that Archer is going to play the role of the Official Air Taxi Provider at the Olympic Games. I see Archer using that publicity as a globally visible anchor. Somewhat of a use case that no peer currently matches. I also expect them to use it as a fixed commercialisation deadline around which the regulators can coordinate.

Risk

One near-term I see for Archer is still around regulatory scrutiny. And that's because of how swift things can change from a rule point of view. Just recently, Congress made the move to tighten aviation safety rules because of a fatal military helicopter crash. Proposed changes to the National Defense Authorisation Act (NDAA) would remove exemptions that currently allow certain aircraft to operate without full ADS-B surveillance. So that would signal a broader shift toward much stricter and more uniform airspace safety requirements. Now, Archer’s eVTOL aircraft already incorporate ADS-B systems, as you know, but the concern for investors is that tougher rules now could indirectly raise certification and operational hurdles, particularly since we’re seeing the industry move toward increasingly autonomous flight capabilities.

Valuation

As you would expect, Archer’s current valuation reflects its status as a pre-revenue, high-growth aviation tech company rather than a traditional industrial firm. The stock is trading around $8.86, and they carry a market cap of roughly $6.5 billion with an enterprise value of about $4.9 billion. They’re also supported by a pretty strong balance sheet, which includes $1.64 billion in cash and only $88.6 million in total debt. Now, because the company is still loss-making, a lot of traditional ratios are not yet meaningful, making asset- and sales-based metrics much more relevant for Archer stock. On that basis, the company trades at about 2.0x forward price to sales and 2.6x forward price to book. I think these are quite reasonable levels relative to other early-stage aerospace and advanced mobility peers, especially given their long runway of cash and improving loss trajectory. Overall, the valuation would suggest investors are pricing in execution and certification progress over the next few years, which should serve as the biggest stock catalyst.

Just to swing back briefly to the competition, Joby's valuation, on the other hand, reflects a much more aggressive growth premium than Archer's. With the stock now trading around $15.43, Joby has a market cap of about $14.1 billion and an enterprise value of about $13.1 billion, despite them still being a pre-revenue company. The most relevant comparison here is sales/asset-based multiples, in which Joby appears very expensive. They trade at around 330x forward EV to sales and over 8.5x forward price-to-book. So this kind of premium tells me investors are pricing in Joby as the long-term category leader between the two, with faster certification, earlier autonomy, and probably more global scaling potential.

Looking ahead, next earnings

Next earnings for Archer are set to come in sometime around February 26th, and I think we should see the same trend typical for this pre-commercial eVTOL developer. Consensus estimates are showing a loss per share of about –$0.23 to –$0.24, which would be a slight improvement compared with the larger losses we’ve seen in prior years. It’s reflective of a bit of incremental progress toward product development and cost discipline, but still well below breakeven. Revenue expectations for the 4Q25 quarter are forecast to be roughly $1.4 million. I also expect many investors/analysts will likely be watching not just the headline numbers, but updates on progress toward certification of the Midnight.

data.title: AMD: Regret Comes Later (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
AMD (AMD) stock has been on a tear in the last 12 months, and while since my last analysis shares are relatively flat, they may indeed still go higher in price in the short term. But that is no reason to get involved right now as a value investor. Markets are overvalued. In the current macroeconomy, based on aggregate market valuation that takes into account 14 market-leading models, the historical statistical likelihood of a positive three-year (let alone five-year) S&P 500 (SP500) return is zero. I'll say that again: historically, since the 1950s, at this market valuation, there have been zero instances of a positive three-year annual return in the S&P 500. This also tracks most of the time when the models are independent and not aggregated. Ladies and gentlemen, that is playing in a game where the house always wins: arguably 100% of the time. The question is, are you the sucker at the table?

If you're asking yourself if this time is different, you're asking the wrong question. You need to be asking yourself, "is this a game I want to play?" In other words, to protect your future, you must know how to analyze correctly. Failure in financial markets to correctly assess situations more often than not leads to ruin; it is the same in life more broadly. And the regret comes later, not when everything feels euphoric, but when the good emotions fade, reality sets in, and we have to live with the often generational consequences of our present perspectives which we wrongly or rightly made decisions on.

When I look at AMD, I see a genuinely remarkable company and CEO. But I also see a crowd of market participants that have projected their value onto the stock to the detriment of the equity's intrinsic valuation being accurately depicted in public markets. This is a shame, but as we know, it is not uncommon.

Truthfully, we're in a capex cycle that is near-peak in terms of equity market sentiment, even if the real-capex peak may be two or three more years away. Given that markets price equities about 12 months prior to real economy activity, we are looking at about one to two years of stock market growth left in the AI supercycle's phase 1, which I call "scarcity overbuild."

What happens next looks like return on investment slippage, weaker utilization, pushouts, and even pricing pressure in cloud services and inference. The capex cycle's equity market downturn does not require a collapse in demand, it simply requires the market's risk-premium to adjust due to the reality of cash economics. When terminal return on invested capital projections reset, valuation multiples will compress. As a result, AMD's stock price will tank hard, and likely become undervalued, before the full utilization economics are proven.

Everything Flashes Green, Until It Doesn't

In every bubble throughout modern-era market history, it's been consensus bullishness right through to the point of collapse. If you want evidence of this, just look at AMD's own consensus Wall Street price targets during the 2021–2022 inflation bubble:

There are several fundamental elements that are likely to cause a value de-rating beyond pure valuation. You need to be monitoring capex leases as a share of operating cash flow, depreciation growth versus revenue, cost per compute function (inference vs. training), and power constraints. Each of which I have discussed in detail in prior analyses on my Seeking Alpha profile page.

Remember, everything flashes green in a bubble because consensus is driving the show. Everybody agrees that the opportunity is ongoing, and next year will bring greater rewards than last. But people who listen to consensus will always end up one way in the end: average. Instead, someone who is truly skilled and attuned to their innate abilities will recognize that the valuation is too high for a stock precisely because consensus is "unwaveringly bullish." If you cannot trust your own perceptions, you will be left following the guidance of others, who may not have your best interests at heart.

Could This Time Be Different?

There are so many issues with the argument that this time is different. We're not looking at an undervalued market (i.e. a market that has changed from being previously overvalued), and making a rational judgement that entry points are now opportune. We are, instead, looking at the same market with the same disastrous symptoms of impending loss and decline, and questioning if factors are materially different. There is a reason that history is the study of statesmen and financiers alike. As Churchill said, "Study history, study history. In history lies all the secrets of statecraft."

From my study of historical cyclical price action that envelopes intrinsic value, I believe the following outlook is most reasonable to assume right now. That is, over the next 9–12 months, AMD stock will test new highs. Following this "melt-up," as it becomes clearer that hyperscalers (who are the primary customers of AMD's data-center semiconductors) will be refining spending in the years ahead, AMD stock will begin to consolidate and then sell off.

I can't predict the exact timing of such a correction; no one can. I have made my best estimates. However, I am confident in the general trend we are likely to witness, which makes this a good time to begin trimming AMD & AI-semi positions, and then accumulate shares again at the next value opportunity once the trough of the correction looks settled.

In the following chart, the white line represents the real price action that I forecast, while the orange line represents real intrinsic value estimated by me.

Q4 Earnings Preview

In such an overvalued macro, no long-term value investor should care more about whether AMD beats Q4 estimates than whether forward demand confirms current certainty. Even if confirmation is provided, management won't be providing guidance at this juncture for two years out, which is when the phase-1 thesis begins to break.

At worst, we're likely looking at in-line results leading to consolidation in price and some nervousness in the broader markets. More likely, we're looking at a mild beat with a moderate raise aligning with prudent optical support. Either way, nothing massive is on the agenda that's public knowledge right now that would force a positive structural re-rate. And with sentiment being full, this is not a moment to invest, only to hold and trim accordingly in the near future.

AMD's Q4 guidance is 25% year-over-year revenue growth and 4% sequentially at the midpoint. Non-GAAP gross margin is forecast at 54.5%, and guidance excluded MI308 shipments to China.

Conclusion: Hold

Have you ever crashed and burned? If you're young, maybe not. If you're old and lucky, maybe not. But the ego always feels the safest, the most enjoyed, just before reality sets in. This is not a time in the markets for complacency. It's a time where you either know clearly what you are doing, or you don't.

The AI boom looks impenetrable. GDP in America is soaring beyond expectations, and capex looks "eternal." If you've any wisdom in you, you will recognize that nothing is eternal, but God.

A consensus rating of +23.5% upside in the next 12 months means nothing in the face of an aggregate market valuation that is more than +2 standard deviations above fair value. It is statistically unknown for this valuation matrix to deliver positive total returns over the next three to five years. Assess the current dynamics how you want, even own a small weight in AI shares, but if you aren't lowering beta right now and raising a cash position, all you are doing is driving with blind optimism down the road to the inevitable crash.

data.title: Hartford Balanced Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US equities advanced in the fourth quarter, registering a sizable return for 2025. Markets were bolstered by robust corporate earnings, resilient consumer spending, and solid economic growth, although anxiety about lofty valuations in the technology sector and concerns surrounding massive AI spending drove capital toward value/cyclical areas of the market. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) reduced interest rates by 50 basis points (bps)[1] during the quarter.

Global fixed-income markets also delivered positive total returns in the fourth quarter of 2025, as policy uncertainty, fiscal developments, and divergent central-bank actions shaped the investment landscape. The US began the quarter with its longest government shutdown on record, which delayed key economic data and forced the Fed to make policy decisions with limited visibility. The shutdown ended in mid-November, but data gaps persisted, adding to market uncertainty. Credit spreads[2] diverged across ratings and regions. In the US, agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and high-yield credit spreads tightened, while investment-grade spreads widened. European spreads broadly narrowed. Both US investment-grade and high-yield posted positive total returns, outperforming European counterparts. Global aggregate and US high-yield indices contributed positively to returns, while government bond returns were more mixed.

Performance Summary

  • Hartford Balanced Income Fund (I share) underperformed its benchmark.
  • Sector allocation was the main driver of relative underperformance, driven by our underweight to communication services and overweights to real estate and utilities, but was partially offset by our overweight to healthcare and underweight to consumer discretionary.
  • Security selection contributed to relative performance, driven by strong selection in healthcare, materials, and financials, and partially offset by selection in information technology.
  • An overweight to and security selection within high-yield credit and an overweight to emerging-markets debt contributed positively to relative returns over the period.
  • Security selection within emerging-markets debt had a slightly negative effect on relative performance.

Positioning & Outlook

  • Our universe remains rich with new opportunities. We continue to focus on finding high quality businesses with strong balance sheets and sustainable dividends. We’re spending a lot of time on our downside stress-test scenarios and are confident in the sustainability of dividends and long-term value of holdings in the portfolio.
  • Within equities, utilities, healthcare, and real estate represented our largest sector overweights relative to the benchmark at quarter-end. We were most underweight to communication services, information technology, and financials.
  • Within the fixed-income portion of the Fund, we maintain our underweight positioning in investment-grade credit as valuations remain relatively rich compared to history. We maintain a modest overweight to emerging markets because we find attractive relative value in this segment of the market.

[1] Basis point is a unit that is equal to 1/100th of 1% and is used to denote the change in a financial instrument. The basis point is commonly used for calculating changes in interest rates, equity indexes and the yield of a fixed-income security

[2] Spreads are the difference in yields between two fixed income securities with the same maturity but originating from different investment sectors.
